rationale_for_baseline_tier: Physiologically stable early pregnancy with an acute situational panic attack that is settling with de-escalation. Risk Level in the source is Low; the disposition is a mandatory GP review in 48 hours with serum beta-hCG, blood group and a dating ultrasound. There is no physical emergency tonight — the tachycardia/tachypnoea are panic-congruent, not shock or ACS. Because the pregnancy's location is unconfirmed and ectopic must be excluded by ultrasound, the correct tier is T2 (prompt GP within 24-48h), NOT routine and NOT an emergency. The panic must be supported, not escalated to ED. A firm ectopic 000/ED safety net is held over the top of the T2 baseline.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe one-sided lower abdominal/pelvic pain, pain referred to the tip of the shoulder, heavy vaginal bleeding, or feeling faint/dizzy on standing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, at any time — do not wait for the morning
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the cardinal features of a ruptured or rupturing ectopic pregnancy — a surgical emergency with risk of catastrophic intra-abdominal haemorrhage. Pregnancy location is unconfirmed, so this safety net is mandatory tonight.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sudden severe pain low in your tummy (especially on one side), pain at the tip of your shoulder, heavy bleeding, or you feel faint when you stand — call 000 or go straight to the nearest Emergency Department. Don't wait until morning.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Escalating, uncontrollable distress overnight, or any thoughts of self-harm
escalation_edges.rationale: A lone, highly distressed patient may deteriorate psychologically; a same-day/after-hours mental-health or crisis pathway is the correct escalation for the distress — not an emergency medical response for the physiological signs.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the distress becomes too much to cope with tonight, call your after-hours GP, a mental-health crisis line, or Lifeline on 13 11 14 — and if you ever feel unsafe, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New chest pain, pain radiating to the arm or jaw, or breathlessness that does NOT settle with slowed breathing
escalation_edges.rationale: Although ACS is excluded on current features, genuinely new persistent cardiorespiratory symptoms that do not fit panic warrant urgent in-person assessment — a conservative backstop.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop real chest pain, pain spreading to your arm or jaw, or breathlessness that doesn't ease when you slow your breathing, go to an Emergency Department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no emotional support present tonight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Strengthen the panic de-escalation and social-support prompt; make the after-hours and crisis-line contacts explicit; ensure she knows exactly who to call overnight.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: There is no physical emergency tonight. Book to see your GP within 48 hours and get the serum beta-hCG blood test done first thing tomorrow morning, along with the dating ultrasound your GP arranges. Tonight, focus on slow breathing and rest, avoid searching online, and don't make any major decisions.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP appointment within 48 hours; pathology first thing tomorrow morning
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If distress becomes unmanageable overnight, contact your after-hours GP service or a mental-health crisis line (e.g. Lifeline 13 11 14). Call 000 or go to an Emergency Department for any sudden severe pelvic pain, shoulder-tip pain, heavy bleeding, or faintness.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth contact for a distressing unexpected positive home pregnancy test; panic attack de-escalated; ectopic screened negative on history but location unconfirmed; advised serum beta-hCG, blood group and antibody screen, and early dating ultrasound; ectopic 000/ED safety net given; GP review in 48h to review results and discuss options.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the encounter as routine/self-care with no prompt confirmation pathway and no ectopic safety net, leaving an unconfirmed-location pregnancy and a lone distressed patient without a plan.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving ectopic goes unrecognised because location was never confirmed and no red-flag safety net was given.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging only a routine (1-2 week) review, delaying serum beta-hCG and dating ultrasound and softening the ectopic safety net.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a physiologically stable patient to ED/000 for the resolved panic attack (misreading panic-congruent tachycardia/tachypnoea as shock or ACS) — the primary over-triage error for this case.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The pregnancy's location is unknown — a positive urine test cannot distinguish intrauterine from ectopic implantation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold the ectopic as an important-not-to-miss possibility, arrange prompt confirmation (serum beta-hCG + dating ultrasound), and give a clear 000/ED red-flag safety net — while reassuring that there is no emergency right now.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either assuming an intrauterine location and dropping the safety net, or resolving the uncertainty by over-triaging the stable patient to the Emergency Department tonight.
